revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ar:غينكاكو-جي]][[arz:جينكاكو جى]][[az:Qinkaku məbədi]][[bar:Ginkaku-ji]][[cs:Ginkakudži]][[de:Ginkaku-ji]][[el:Γκινκάκου-τζι]][[en:Ginkaku-ji]][[eo:Jishōji]][[es:Ginkaku-ji]][[eu:Ginkaku-ji]][[fa:گینکاکو-جی]][[fi:Ginkaku-ji]][[fr:Ginkaku-ji]][[hak:Ginkakuji]][[hu:Ginkakudzsi]][[id:Ginkaku-ji]][[it:Ginkaku-ji]][[ja:慈照寺]][[ko:지쇼지]][[my:ဂင်ခခုဂျိ]][[nl:Ginkaku-ji]][[pl:Srebrny Pawilon]][[pt:Ginkaku-ji]][[ru:Гинкаку-дзи]][[sv:Ginkaku-ji]][[th:กิงกากูจิ]][[tr:Ginkaku-ji]][[uk:Монастир Ґінкаку]][[vi:Ginkakuji]][[zh:慈照寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:GinkakujiTemple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shōkoku-ji school|qid=Q126510501|1=Shōkoku-ji school}}|prefecture={{ill|Sakyō-ku|qid=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.026666666667|N|135.79833333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}|part_of={{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|qid=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}}} {{Short description|Zen buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Jishō-ji | native_name = 慈照寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Ginkakuji_Kyoto03-r.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = The ''kannonden'' at Jishō-ji,
commonly known as the Silver Pavilion (''Ginkaku'') {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=220}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 2 Ginkakuji-chō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyōto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}}, {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} sect, {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|qid=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}} school | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = [https://www.shokoku-ji.jp/ginkakuji/ Japanese Website] | founded_by = {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}} | year_completed = 1490 }} {{nihongo|'''Ginkaku-ji'''|銀閣寺||lit. "Temple of the Silver Pavilion"}}, officially named {{nihongo|'''Jishō-ji'''|慈照寺||lit. "Temple of Shining Mercy"}}, is a Zen temple in the [[:en:Sakyo-ku, Kyoto|Sakyo ward]] of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is one of the constructions that represent the [[:en:Higashiyama Culture|Higashiyama Culture]] of the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. == History == [[File:Sunlight through clouds and view of Ginkaku-ji Temple from above, Kyoto, Japan.jpg|left|thumb|View of Ginkaku-ji and Tōgudō]]{{More citations needed section|date=December 2023}} {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}} initiated plans for creating a retirement villa and gardens as early as 1460, and it functioned as a pleasure villa for the shoguns to rest from their administrative duties. After his death, Yoshimasa arranged for this property to become a Zen temple under the name '''Jishō-ji'''.{{Cite book |last=Deal |first=William E. |title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=2006 |location=USA |pages=214 |language=en}} The temple is today associated with the [[:en:Shokoku-ji|Shokoku-ji]] branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} Zen. The two-storied {{nihongo|''Kannon-den''|観音殿||[[:en:Kuan Yin|Kannon]] hall}}, is the main temple structure. Its construction began February 21, 1482 (''Bummei 14, fourth day of the second month'').Keene, Donald. (2003). [https://books.google.com/books?id=xtPsa5zI8jcC&dq=ginkaku&pg=PA87 ''Yoshimasa and the Silver Pavilion,'' p. 87.] For the structure's design, Yoshimasa sought to emulate the golden [[Kinkaku-ji]], which had been commissioned by his grandfather {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|qid=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}. It is popularly known as ''Ginkaku'', the "Silver Pavilion," because of the initial plans to cover its exterior in silver foil, but this familiar nickname dates back only as far as the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1600–1868).Keene, [https://books.google.com/books?id=xtPsa5zI8jcC&dq=ginkaku&pg=PA88 p. 88.] During the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, construction was halted. Despite Yoshimasa's intention to cover the structure with a distinctive silver foil overlay, this work was delayed so long that the plans were never realized before Yoshimasa's death. The present appearance of the structure is understood to be the same as when Yoshimasa himself last saw it. This "unfinished" appearance illustrates one of the aspects of "[[wabi-sabi]]" quality.[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2008jan/01-23.html "Protecting Ginkaku-ji, the Beauty of Wabi-sabi; Reluctance to Black Lacquering the Outer Wall,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303204303/http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2008jan/01-23.html|date=2016-03-03}} ''Kyoto Shimbun.'' January 23, 2008. Like Kinkaku-ji, Ginkaku-ji was originally built to serve as a place of rest and solitude for the Shōgun. During his reign as Shōgun, Ashikaga Yoshimasa inspired a new outpouring of traditional culture, which came to be known as ''[[:en:Higashiyama Bunka|Higashiyama Bunka]]'' (the Culture of the Eastern Mountain). Having retired to the villa, it is said Yoshimasa sat in the pavilion, contemplating the calm and beauty of the gardens as the Ōnin War worsened and Kyoto was burned to the ground. In 1485, Yoshimasa became a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} Buddhist monk. After his death on January 27, 1490 (''Entoku 2, seventh day of the first month''),Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 p. 361.] the villa and gardens became a Buddhist temple complex, renamed Jishō-ji after Yoshimasa's Buddhist name. After extensive restoration, which started in February 2008, Ginkaku-ji is again in full glory to visit. The garden and temple complex are open to the public. There is still no silver foil used. After much discussion, it was decided not to refinish the lacquer to the original state.{{Citation needed|reason=Who discussed this and who decided?|date=April 2019}} The lacquer finish was the source of the original silver appearance of the temple, with the reflection of the silver water of the pond on the lacquer finish. == Garden == {{More citations needed section|date=December 2023}} In addition to the temple's famous building, the property features wooded grounds covered with a variety of mosses. This {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|qid=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} was supposedly designed by the great landscape artist {{ill|Sōami|en|Sōami|qid=Q2746002|lt=Sōami|1=Sōami}}. In the later periods of Japanese garden history, fine stones were transported from a ruined or impoverished garden to a new one. According to the book ''Landscape design: A Cultural and Architectural History'' by Elizabeth Barlow Rogers, "In 1474, following his retirement from the shogunate, Yoshimasa (1436-1490), had some of the stones and pine trees of the Flowery Palace and Muromachi Hall, both of which had been devastated by civil warfare, removed to his villa retreat at the base of Higashiyama (the Eastern Hills). There he lived from 1438 until his death, when the villa was converted to the Zen temple, Jisho-ji, or Ginkakuji (the Temple of the Silver Pavilion), as it is better known." The Zen culture is not entirely represented in this garden because it was financed by the shogunate as a retreat and the primary use was aesthetic enjoyment. Instead of being designed by and for the use of monks, practitioners of zazen, or seated meditation. Ginkakuji was constructed in relation to its surroundings. This is described in the ''Journal of Asian Studies'' by Ichito Ishida and Delmer M. Brown, "The southeast corner of the first floor has openings in the walls, since a pond is located on that corner of the building, beyond which the moon rises between the peaks of Higashiyama. And since a lake extending northeastward reflected light that suffices even for reading, the room on the northeast corner has been planned as a library. Therefore, the natural objects do not merely surround the building, twisting it out of shape but supply intrinsic motivation for the structural design." The sand garden of Ginkaku-ji has become particularly well known; and the carefully formed pile of sand which is said to symbolize {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} is an essential element in the garden. == Gallery == File:Ginkakuji01.jpg|Entrance way of the temple File:Ginkakuji04.jpg|Ginkaku-ji temple on a snowy day File:Ginkakuji 2012.jpg|View from the pond in autumn File:Ginkakuji Temple Togudo 2009 059.jpg|Tōgu-dō (1486), a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} File:Ginkaku-ji, Togudo Hall, November 2016.jpg|Tōgudō in autumn File:Dry-sand Zen garden made of beige stripes a sunny day at Higashiyama Jisho-ji Buddhist temple Ginkaku-ji Kyoto Japan.jpg|''Ginshadan'' File:Kyoto Silver Pavilion Pond.jpg|''Kannon-den'' or ''"Kannon Bodhisattva Hall"'' File:Ginkaku-ji, Kyoto.jpg|Kōgetsudai == See also == {{Portal|Japan|Religion|Gardening|Architecture}} * {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|qid=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|qid=Q254677|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)}} * {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|qid=Q20982902|lt=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} * {{ill|Tourism in Japan|en|Tourism in Japan|qid=Q1060220|lt=Tourism in Japan|1=Tourism in Japan}} == Notes == {{reflist|2}} == References == * {{cite book |last1=Keene |first1=D. |author-link=Donald Keene |date=2003 |title=Yoshimasa and the Silver Pavilion: The Creation of the Soul of Japan |url=https://worldcat.org/en/title/213305859 |location=New York |publisher=[[Columbia University Press]] |isbn=9780231503860 |oclc=213305859 |access-date=May 30, 2023}} * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|qid=Q332779|lt=Titsingh|1=Isaac Titsingh}}, Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/{{ill|Hayashi Gahō|en|Hayashi Gahō|qid=Q3128811|lt=Hayashi Gahō|1=Hayashi Gahō}}, 1652], ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. * {{cite journal |last1=Ishida |first1=I.|last2=Brown |first2=D. M. |date=1963 |title=Zen Buddhism and Muromachi Art |url= https://www.jstor.org/stable/2049856 |journal=The Journal of Asian Studies |volume=22 |issue=4 |pages=417–432 |doi=10.2307/2049856 |access-date=May 30, 2023}} == Further reading == *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2= Young |first2= Michiko |title= The Art of the Japanese Garden |location= Singapore |publisher= Tuttle |date= 2005 |type= Hardback |isbn=978-0-8048-3598-5}} == External links == * [https://www.shokoku-ji.jp/en/ginkakuji/ Official Website] {{in lang|en}} {{Commons Category|Ginkaku-ji}} *[https://www.worldhistory.org/Ginkakuji/ World History Encyclopedia - Ginkakuji] *{{Osmway|105817561}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|35|01|36|N|135|47|54|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=title}} [[en:Ginkaku-ji]] [[ar:غينكاكو-جي]] [[arz:جينكاكو جى]] [[az:Qinkaku məbədi]] [[bar:Ginkaku-ji]] {{Commons category|Ginkaku-ji}} [[cs:Ginkakudži]] [[de:Ginkaku-ji]] [[el:Γκινκάκου-τζι]] [[eo:Jishōji]] [[es:Ginkaku-ji]] [[eu:Ginkaku-ji]] [[fa:گینکاکو-جی]] [[fi:Ginkaku-ji]] [[fr:Ginkaku-ji]] [[hak:Ginkakuji]] [[hu:Ginkakudzsi]] [[id:Ginkaku-ji]] [[it:Ginkaku-ji]] [[ja:慈照寺]] [[ko:지쇼지]] [[my:ဂင်ခခုဂျိ]] [[nl:Ginkaku-ji]] [[pl:Srebrny Pawilon]] [[pt:Ginkaku-ji]] [[ru:Гинкаку-дзи]] [[sv:Ginkaku-ji]] [[th:กิงกากูจิ]] [[tr:Ginkaku-ji]] [[uk:Монастир Ґінкаку]] [[vi:Ginkakuji]] [[zh:慈照寺]] {{wikidata link|Q257473}} [[Category:1470s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1474 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]]
